event: The Deliverance of Saint Peter from prison
symbols: angel — divine messenger sent by God to deliver His servant from the power of human authority
chains on Peter's wrists — imprisonment and persecution of the faithful, bondage broken by divine power
angel's upward-pointing finger — directing Peter's attention heavenward, toward the source of his deliverance
angel's touch on Peter's shoulder — the gentle awakening described in Acts 12:7, divine tenderness in rescue
darkness — the prison of death, sin, and worldly oppression from which God rescues His people
light emanating from the angel — the glory of God that pierces the darkness of captivity (Acts 12:7)
description: A dramatic chiaroscuro scene showing two figures in intimate proximity against a deeply dark background. At left, a youthful angel with curly auburn hair and visible wings leans toward an elderly bearded man, touching his shoulder with one hand. The angel wears a red draped garment and points upward with the index finger of his raised right hand. The elderly man — Saint Peter — looks upward with an expression of astonishment and dawning wonder, his mouth slightly open, white hair and beard illuminated. His wrists bear chains.
theological_description: The Deliverance of Saint Peter depicts the miraculous event recorded in Acts 12:1-19, when King Herod Agrippa I imprisoned Peter during the Feast of Unleavened Bread, intending to execute him after Passover. While Peter slept between two soldiers, bound with chains, an angel of the Lord appeared, filled the cell with light, struck Peter on the side to wake him, and commanded him to rise. The chains fell from his wrists, and the angel led him past the guards and through the iron gate, which opened of its own accord. This deliverance is one of the great typological events of the New Testament, echoing Israel's liberation from Egypt — Peter's rescue during Passover explicitly parallels the Exodus. The angel pointing upward directs Peter (and the viewer) to recognize God as the author of this liberation. The scene embodies the theology of divine sovereignty over earthly powers: Herod, representing the might of Rome, cannot hold what God intends to free. Psalm 146:7 declares that the Lord 'sets prisoners free,' and this is its New Testament fulfillment. Peter's astonished gaze reflects Acts 12:9, where 'he did not know that what was being done by the angel was real, but thought he was seeing a vision.' The early Church's prayer for Peter (Acts 12:5) demonstrates the power of intercessory prayer, and God's dramatic answer affirms that no human authority can thwart divine purposes.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct biblical narrative: Herod imprisons Peter, an angel appears in the night, breaks his chains, and leads him to freedom.
The specific moment depicted: the angel strikes Peter's side, light fills the cell, and the chains fall from his wrists.
The Church prays earnestly for Peter — the theological context of intercessory prayer answered by miraculous deliverance.
The Lord sets prisoners free — Peter's deliverance fulfills this psalm of divine liberation.
Those who sat in darkness and the shadow of death, prisoners in affliction — God broke their bonds and delivered them.
An earlier angelic prison deliverance of the apostles, establishing the pattern of God liberating His witnesses.
The Servant of the Lord is commissioned to open the eyes of the blind and free captives from prison — fulfilled in Christ's ministry through His apostles.
